post.author: Story Makers
post.caption: Story Makers · FollowStory Makers · Original audioThe little girl appeared beside the biker’s booth so quietly that he almost didn’t notice her until she whispered.“Sir…”He turned mid-bite, still holding his fork, and saw a tiny girl in an oversized yellow t-shirt standing in the dusty diner light. Her cheeks were dirty, her hair was tangled, and her eyes kept jumping toward the young man at the counter.The biker’s face softened.“Hey… you okay?”The girl leaned closer to his ear, trembling so badly her voice barely came out.“That’s not my dad.”The diner went silent in his mind before the room actually did.The biker’s jaw tightened. He gently pulled her into the booth beside him and placed one heavy arm in front of her like a wall.“Stay behind me.”Across the diner, the young man at the counter slowly turned around.The biker stood, leather vest creaking, chair scraping hard against the floor.“We need to talk.”The girl clutched his vest, then suddenly froze at the wolf patch sewn onto the leather. Her eyes filled with tears.“Mom said… if I ever saw that patch… I should run to you.”The biker stopped breathing.His voice dropped.“What’s your mama’s name?”The girl looked at the man at the counter, then whispered:“Rose.”The biker lifted his eyes toward the young man—👉 Part 2 in the comments See less ::-webkit-scrollbar { -webkit-appearance: none; width: 7px; } ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb { border-radius: 4px; background: var(--scroll-thumb); }924K6.7K8.8K7181516

article.title: 🎬 Part 2: The young man at the counter smiled like he still thought he could walk out.
article.content:
The young man at the counter smiled like he still thought he could walk out.

But the biker’s face had changed completely.

Rose was not just a name to him. It was a wound that had never closed.

He looked down at the little girl, then back at the man.

“Where is her mother?”

The young man shrugged. “She gave the kid to me.”

The girl shook her head violently, hiding behind the biker’s vest.

“He’s lying. He took me when Mom screamed.”

Every biker in the diner stood up at once.

The door chime rang as two more men in leather stepped inside, blocking the exit without saying a word.

The biker reached into his vest and pulled out an old photo of a young woman wearing the same wolf patch on a necklace.

The little girl touched the picture.

“That’s Mom.”

The biker’s eyes filled with rage.

The young man stepped back.

The biker’s voice went cold.

“Rose is my sister.”

Then the little girl whispered:

“She’s still in his car.”
